critic_raw: The design handles the temporal structure well. I want to probe the 60/20/20 split ratio for a 3-5 year dataset. With 3 years of data, the validation set is 7.2 months and the test set is 7.2 months. With 5 years, validation is 12 months and test is 12 months. The range of test window sizes (7-12 months) means the results are somewhat dependent on the dataset length chosen. If the grid operator's 3-year dataset is used, the test period is only about 7 months — not a full year cycle. The design claims 'multi-season evaluation: the test window spans at least 12 months' but with 3 years of total data, this is not achievable. Additionally, the TFT's 7-day lookback window (168 hours) means the model's input at the start of the test period sees training data only — but this creates an implicit warm-up period at the test boundary that is not accounted for.
defender_raw: On test window length: the design uses '3-5 years of hourly load data' — the 12-month test window requirement for multi-season evaluation implies the design needs at least 5 years of data to achieve a 12-month test period with the 60/20/20 split. With 3 years, the test would be only ~7 months. However, Section 7 explicitly addresses this: 'The test window spans at least 12 months to ensure improvement is robust across seasonal variation.' This requirement implies the grid operator must provide sufficient data. If 5 years is not available, the scope of claims would be limited. On the warm-up period at the test boundary: the 7-day lookback at the start of the test period uses the last 7 days of validation data — this is normal and correct behavior. The model has already been trained; no retraining occurs. The first few test predictions have valid lookback windows from the validation period.
adjudication_raw: Two concerns: test window length with minimum data and lookback warm-up at test boundary. Both addressed: design specifies 12-month test as a requirement (implying 5-year data needed); lookback at test start uses validation period data, which is correct. Verdict: defense_wins.
